plainMeaning: The Navkāra's closing line — 'this is the foremost of all blessings' — recited as sealed with the word svāhā, is set like a diamond lid over the fortress wall, roofing and guarding the reciter's body.
recitation: svāhāntaṁ ca padaṁ jñeyaṁ, paḍhamaṁ hoi maṅgalaṁ, / vapropari vajamayaṁ, pidhānaṁ deha-rakṣaṇo.

wordByWord.gloss: ending in (the exclamation) svāhā — i.e. the pada recited with a concluding svāhā
wordByWord.etymology: svāhā (ritual sealing-exclamation) + anta ('end'); bahuvrīhi 'that which has svāhā for its end', acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: Marks this line as recited like a mantric oblation, capped by svāhā; a stotra-specific ritual instruction, not part of the bare Navkāra.
Adds this final element to the fortification scheme begun in the preceding verses.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pada, n., 'word, line, metrical foot'; acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: Here the concluding line of the Navkāra (one of the nava-pada).

wordByWord.etymology: gerundive (kṛtya) of √jñā 'to know'; n. acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: Prescriptive: the reciter 'should know' this pada in its svāhā-sealed form.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paḍhama < Skt. prathama 'first'; n. nom./acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: Quotes the Navkāra's closing chūlikā line verbatim.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit hoi < Skt. bhavati '(it) is/becomes'; 3rd sg. pres.
wordByWord.notes: Variant of the more common 'havai' in the standard chūlikā 'paḍhamaṁ havai maṅgalaṁ'.
Completes the cited line: 'paḍhamaṁ hoi maṅgalaṁ' = 'it is the foremost blessing'.

wordByWord.etymology: vapra ('rampart, fortress-wall') + upari ('above, upon'); sandhi a+u → o.
wordByWord.notes: Refers back to the outer rampart raised in v5; the lid is set on top of it.

wordByWord.etymology: vaja (book's verbatim spelling of vajra 'diamond/thunderbolt') + -maya ('made of'); n. acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: Book reads વજ (vaja) for vajra throughout — preserved verbatim.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pidhāna, n. (api + √dhā 'to place over'), 'cover, lid'; nom./acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: The roofing element that seals the fortress from above.

wordByWord.gloss: for the protection of the body
wordByWord.etymology: deha ('body') + rakṣaṇa ('guarding'); tatpuruṣa; here in purposive sense.
wordByWord.notes: End-form -ṇo for expected -ṇaṁ/-ṇaḥ is metrical/orthographic; states the lid's protective function.

literalTranslation: And the pada ending in svāhā is to be known — 'paḍhamaṁ hoi maṅgalaṁ' ('it is the foremost auspicious thing'); upon the rampart, [it is] the adamantine lid, for the protection of the body.
idiomaticTranslation: Know, too, the line sealed with svāhā — 'this is the first and foremost of all blessings'; set as a diamond lid upon the rampart, it roofs the fortress and guards the body within.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse lays the final stone of the armouring. Where v5 raised the outer rampart (vapra) and ringed it with a moat of khadira-embers, v6 now roofs the enclosure: the Navkāra's closing chūlikā line, 'paḍhamaṁ hoi maṅgalaṁ' — 'this is the foremost of all blessings' — becomes the adamantine lid (pidhāna) laid over the wall, for deha-rakṣaṇa, 'protection of the body.' With the floor-slab (v4), the four walls (v5) and now this overhead lid, the diamond enclosure is sealed on every side; the warrior-reciter is wholly within. Two details repay attention. First, this is the only pada the hymn marks svāhāntaṁ, 'ending in svāhā': the composer instructs that the line be recited capped by the ritual sealing-exclamation, transforming the recited words into a mantric oblation that empowers and 'closes' the formula — fittingly, since this line itself closes the Navkāra. Second, the choice of the maṅgala-line as the roof is deliberate: the supreme auspicious thing is set as the supreme covering, so that the protection's apex is its blessing. The verse thus marries verbal auspiciousness to spatial completion — the last word of the mantra becomes the last surface of the fortress.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on the Jain doctrine that the Pañca-Namaskāra is the highest maṅgala — 'maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ, paḍhamaṁ havai maṅgalaṁ,' echoing the Cattāri-maṅgala formula in which the arhats, siddhas, sādhus and the Jina-taught dharma are the four auspicious things. By making this very claim the protective lid, the hymn asserts that auspiciousness (maṅgala) is itself a shielding power: dharma-as-blessing functions as dharma-as-armour. The svāhānta marking shows the text consciously adopting a mantra-ritual idiom (svāhā being the standard oblational seal) to charge a salutational pada — a recognised current in Jain stotra-literature where the Navkāra is wielded as a rakṣā-mantra.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: In recitation the practitioner, having walled himself in at v5, now visualises a flawless diamond slab sliding shut overhead, mentally appending svāhā to 'paḍhamaṁ hoi maṅgalaṁ' as he seals it. With this the enclosure is complete on all six sides, and he rests in a felt sense of total, roofed-over safety — the cue to settle into the protected stillness the closing verses promise.

crossReferences.text: Navkāra chūlikā: 'Eso pañca-namukkāro, savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo / maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ, paḍhamaṁ havai maṅgalaṁ'
Cattāri-maṅgalaṁ — the four auspicious things (arhat, siddha, sādhu, kevali-paṇṇatto dhammo)
'amṛtāpidhānam asi svāhā' — pidhāna paired with svāhā as a ritual seal
crossReferences.source: classical usage cited s.v. pidhāna, Apte, Practical Sanskrit-English Dictionary

sources.consultedFor: Verifying the closing line 'paḍhamaṁ havai maṅgalaṁ' as the Navkāra's chūlikā/maṅgala portion and its history (Dhaky).

sources.consultedFor: Gloss and etymology of pidhāna ('lid/covering') and its classical pairing with svāhā.

sources.title: Vapra — Sanskrit dictionary and Jain/Purāṇic usage ('rampart, fortress-wall')
sources.consultedFor: Gloss and etymology of vapra ('rampart') underlying vapropari.

sources.title: The Namokar Mantra — Prayer of the Jain Religion (JAINA)
sources.consultedFor: Standard English rendering of the closing maṅgala lines of the Navkāra.

notes: Verse text preserved verbatim from the book, including વજ (vaja) for vajra and the end-form દેહરક્ષણો (deha-rakṣaṇo, for expected -ṇaṁ/-ṇaḥ, metrical). 'હોઇ' (hoi) is a variant of the more usual 'havai' in the standard chūlikā 'paḍhamaṁ havai maṅgalaṁ'. The svāhānta ('ending in svāhā') instruction is specific to this protective stotra and is not part of the bare Navkāra; I read 'svāhāntaṁ ca padaṁ jñeyaṁ' as a recitation directive (recite the pada sealed with svāhā), which the consulted sources support indirectly via the pidhāna+svāhā ritual idiom but is not independently attested for this exact line — flagged as the main interpretive choice. The source text shows no footnote pāṭhāntara for this verse in the supplied material.
